region_of_origin: South India; widely served in Singapore and Malaysia
food_subgroup: Fermented rice and lentil crepes with spiced potato filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general dosa entry (FOODON_00002572). Butter Masala Dosa is a specific variant with spiced potato filling and butter; closest_parent used as no exact entry exists for this variant.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers dosa as a category. Butter Masala Dosa is a filled and buttered variant; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for dosa or masala dosa as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Butter Masala Dosa is approximately 200 g, comprising one large crisp crepe filled with spiced potato masala and finished with a pat of butter, as typically served at South Indian restaurants in Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: Masala Dosa — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition); butter contribution estimated from USDA FoodData Central (Butter, salted, FDC ID 173410)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides nutrient data for plain masala dosa per 100 g. Butter Masala Dosa adds approximately 5 g butter per 100 g of finished product. Macros adjusted proportionally: butter contribution (energy ~36 kcal, fat ~4.1 g, saturated fat ~2.6 g per 100 g of dish) added to ICMR-NIN masala dosa baseline. Final per-100 g values represent the composite dish as typically served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of South Indian fermented rice-lentil foods — analogous estimate based on plain dosa GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Butter Masala Dosa. GI of 55 is estimated from plain dosa (GI ~55, low category) as reported in the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al. 2008) and corroborated by the University of Sydney GI Database. The addition of butter lowers the effective glycemic response slightly due to fat-mediated gastric emptying delay; however, the potato masala filling raises it. Net GI is conservatively retained at 55 (low). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (55 × 26.5 × 2.00) / 100 = 29.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~280 mg per 100 g; ~560 mg per standard 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one dosa and request reduced salt preparation, as a standard serving contributes approximately 560 mg sodium — about 24% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) but moderate glycemic load (~29 per serving) driven by refined rice batter and potato filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Butter Masala Dosa in controlled portions, pair it with a high-fibre side such as sambar, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as while the GI is low, the moderate glycemic load per serving can still cause a meaningful rise in blood sugar, especially if portions are large.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (~3.1 g per 100 g; ~6.2 g per serving) and moderate sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Butter Masala Dosa to occasional consumption, as the butter contributes approximately 6 g of saturated fat per serving — about 30% of the 20 g daily limit — which may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Masala Dosa (also spelled Butter Masala Dosai or Thosai) is a popular South Indian dish consisting of a large, thin, crispy crepe made from a fermented batter of rice and urad dal (black gram lentil), filled with a spiced potato masala and finished with a generous pat of butter on the surface. The batter is fermented overnight, which gives the dosa its characteristic slight sourness and improves its digestibility. The potato filling — known as masala — is prepared by sautéing boiled potatoes with mustard seeds, curry leaves, onion, green chilli, and turmeric in oil, producing a fragrant, mildly spiced stuffing. The dosa is cooked on a flat iron griddle (tawa) until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining soft inside. The addition of butter distinguishes this variant from a plain masala dosa, adding richness and a slightly higher fat content. Butter Masala Dosa is a staple at South Indian restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia, where it is commonly served with coconut chutney and sambar (a lentil-based vegetable stew). Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~175 kcal per 100 g), moderate carbohydrates from rice and potato, modest protein from lentils, and dietary fibre. The dish has a low glycemic index (~55) but a moderate glycemic load per standard serving, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The butter content raises saturated fat, which is a consideration for cardiovascular health. It is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian but not vegan due to the butter.
common_user_queries: How many calories in butter masala dosa?
Is butter masala dosa gluten free?
Is butter masala dosa good for diabetics?
What is the difference between masala dosa and butter masala dosa?
Can I eat butter masala dosa if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Butter Masala Dosa exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain masala dosa data with butter contribution calculated from USDA FoodData Central. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (plain dosa) in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium due to the adaptation rather than a direct match.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 2. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for masala dosa. 3. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from the butter and cooking oil components using USDA FoodData Central data; these are approximate. 4. The input record uses the spelling 'Thosal' (a Tamil-influenced romanization common in Singapore and Malaysia); the canonical English food name is standardized to 'Butter Masala Dosa' with 'Thosal', 'Thosai', and 'Dosai' retained as aliases. 5. The food is tagged as Malaysian in the input source (field survey data), but the dish is of South Indian origin and is widely served in both Singapore and Malaysia by Indian restaurants; cuisine is recorded as 'South Indian / Malaysian Indian'. 6. GI glycemic load calculation: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 26.5 × 2.00) / 100 = 29.1 per 200 g serving.

Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
Confirmed all ontology match_type values use only allowed strings: exact, closest_parent, none.
